title: SEGMENT 14 — The two axes: developmental progress and morphological similarity

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — The two axes: developmental progress and morphological similarity

I want to give you a conceptual framework that's been useful, both in the literature and in my own thinking, for sorting out what kinds of synthetic embryos there are and how each kind should be treated.
The framework involves two axes.
Axis one: developmental progress.
How far along the developmental timeline does the structure go?
An embryoid body that just sits there as a clump of differentiating cells is at one end.
A blastoid that recapitulates the blastocyst stage is somewhat further along.
An ETX-style early post-implantation model is further still.
A Hanna-style synthetic embryo with a beating heart is much further.
And at the far end, hypothetically, would be a fully gestated synthetic embryo carried to viability.
The axis runs from "barely organized" to "complete development.
Axis two: morphological similarity.
How closely does the structure resemble a real embryo at the developmental stage it occupies?
You can have a blastoid that's a recognizable but somewhat off blastocyst, or a blastoid that's almost indistinguishable from a real blastocyst under the microscope.
You can have a post-implantation structure that has roughly the right organization but with notable drift, or one that's a near-perfect copy.
The axis runs from "loosely embryo-shaped" to "indistinguishable from a real embryo.
These two axes are independent.
You can have a structure that covers a lot of developmental progress with relatively low morphological fidelity — a sort of "long and sloppy" model.
You can have a structure that covers little developmental progress but with very high fidelity — a "short and sharp" model.
Most synthetic embryos sit somewhere in the middle, and as the technology improves, the frontier moves toward the upper right of the diagram: more developmental progress, with higher fidelity.
This framework helps because it gives us a vocabulary for talking about which structures should be subject to which kinds of oversight.
Roughly, the further along both axes you go, the more the structure starts to look like the thing real-embryo ethics was originally designed to regulate.
A clump of differentiating iPSCs that doesn't organize into anything embryo-like is not subject to embryo research rules.
It's just a cell culture experiment.

A blastoid that recapitulates blastocyst-stage organization with moderate fidelity is starting to engage embryo research rules, but with relatively limited concern because it's at an early stage that, even in real embryos, doesn't trigger the most stringent ethical limits.
An ETX-style structure that's getting close to gastrulation, with high fidelity, is engaging the central ethical concerns of the 14-day rule directly.
A hypothetical future synthetic embryo that reproduces organogenesis with high fidelity is engaging concerns that the 14-day rule didn't even contemplate.
This is, I think, the practical way the field is going to evolve regulatorily.
Not "synthetic embryos are or aren't embryos" as a binary, but a graded set of categories based on where along these two axes a particular construct sits.

The ISSCR guidelines are already moving in this direction — they distinguish between "integrated" embryo models (with all the major lineages) and "non-integrated" ones (missing some), and they emphasize developmental milestones over construct identity.
Let me suggest a specific question to ask about any synthetic embryo construct, which captures both axes:
At what real-embryo developmental stage does this construct sit, and how convincingly?
If the answer is "at the morula stage, very loosely" — there's not much ethical concern, and not much practical use.
If it's "at the blastocyst stage, very convincingly" — there's modest ethical concern (because blastocyst stage in real embryos has accepted research uses), and there are real applications.
If it's "at the gastrulation stage, very convincingly" — there's substantial ethical concern (because this is the stage the 14-day rule was protecting), and there are very valuable applications.

If it's "at organogenesis stage, very convincingly" — there's intense ethical concern, and the construct is approaching a developmental status that, in real embryos, would clearly be a moral patient by most ethical frameworks.
The framework also helps clarify a confusion I sometimes see in popular coverage.
People sometimes argue that synthetic embryos can't be "real" embryos because they're imperfect, or because they can't be implanted into a uterus and gestated, or because they have anatomical drifts.
These are all morphological-fidelity arguments — they're saying the structures are low on Axis Two.
But Axis One — developmental progress — is independent.
A synthetic embryo that reaches the gastrulation stage, even with significant morphological drift, is still recapitulating events that, in real embryos, occur after the traditional 14-day limit.
The fidelity argument doesn't make the developmental-progress concern go away.
Conversely, people sometimes argue that any synthetic embryo is a moral problem because it might be an embryo.
This is an Axis-Two argument that ignores Axis One.

A construct that's morphologically embryo-like but doesn't recapitulate any significant developmental progress is, in most reasonable views, less ethically charged than one that pushes deep into gastrulation.
The two-axis framework forces both questions to be considered together.
That's why I find it useful.
One more observation.
The frontier of the field is moving toward the upper right corner of the diagram.
Better protocols, better culture systems, better cell sources, better understanding of the signaling are all pushing both fidelity and developmental progress upward.
Five years ago, the cutting edge was blastoid-stage with moderate fidelity.
Today, the cutting edge is post-implantation stage with moderate-to-high fidelity in humans, and well into organogenesis in mouse.
In five more years, it's reasonable to expect human models will push further into post-implantation and possibly into early organogenesis stages, with higher fidelity.
This trajectory matters because it means the ethical concerns are not static.
What's acceptable today, with today's limitations, may need to be reconsidered tomorrow as the constructs improve.
The 2021 ISSCR guidelines were written with this in mind — they're designed to be updated as the technology evolves.
The question of whether the updates will happen quickly enough, and whether they'll be stringent enough to match the technical capabilities, is part of what the field is wrestling with.
